attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-month history of painless, generalised lymphadenopathy, fatigue, night sweats, and pruritus. She reports no recent infections or travel. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, and inguinal regions. The lymph nodes are firm, non-tender, and range in size from 2-3 cm. No hepatosplenomegaly is noted.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of lymphoma including painless lymphadenopathy, constitutional B symptoms (night sweats and fatigue), pruritus, and a chronic four-month history. The combination of generalised lymphadenopathy with B symptoms and pruritus in a young adult strongly suggests Hodgkin lymphoma.

Hodgkin lymphoma commonly presents in young adults with painless lymphadenopathy and B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) in approximately 30% of cases. Pruritus is a characteristic symptom that occurs in about 10-15% of patients with Hodgkin lymphoma and may precede other symptoms. The diagnosis is confirmed by lymph node biopsy showing Reed-Sternberg cells surrounded by a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. The mixed cellularity subtype is often associated with Epstein-Barr virus infection.

Non-Hodgkin lymphoma encompasses a diverse group of lymphomas that can present with similar clinical features including lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms. However, pruritus is less common in non-Hodgkin lymphoma compared to Hodgkin lymphoma. The definitive distinction requires histopathological examination showing the absence of Reed-Sternberg cells.

Infectious mononucleosis is commonly caused by Epstein-Barr virus and typically presents more acutely with pharyngitis, fever, and tender lymphadenopathy. The four-month duration and presence of pruritus make this diagnosis unlikely. Additionally, infectious mononucleosis usually resolves within weeks rather than persisting for months.

Sarcoidosis is a systemic granulomatous disease that can involve lymph nodes and present with fatigue. However, pruritus is not a typical feature of sarcoidosis. The condition more commonly presents with hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary involvement, and sometimes skin lesions or ocular symptoms.

Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disorder that can present with fatigue and lymphadenopathy. However, the lymphadenopathy in lupus is typically reactive and smaller. Patients with lupus usually have other features such as malar rash, arthritis, photosensitivity, or renal involvement, along with positive autoantibodies. Pruritus is not a characteristic feature of lupus.
learningPoints: B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) are present in approximately 30% of patients with Hodgkin lymphoma.
Pruritus is a characteristic symptom that occurs in about 10-15% of patients with Hodgkin lymphoma and may precede other symptoms
references: Ungprasert P, Ryu JH, Matteson EL. Clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment of sarcoidosis. Mayo Clinic Proceedings: Innovations, Quality & Outcomes. 2019 Sep 1;3(3):358-75. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31485575/

Ansell SM. Hodgkin lymphoma: 2025 update on diagnosis, risk-stratification, and management. Am J Hematol. 2024 Dec;99(12):2367-2378. doi: 10.1002/ajh.27470. Epub 2024 Sep 6. PMID: 39239794. Available from: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ajh.27470
